speaker: interviewer
text: Today is June 4, 1996. I'm interviewing Michael Kraus. My name is Jill Greenberg. We are in Brookline, Massachusetts in the United States of America. And this interview is being conducted in English.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number five.

speaker: interviewer
text: Tape number five.

speaker: interviewee
text: We left Mauthausen and walked down from the high ground, where the stone fortress enclosing the camp stood, towards the city that sits on the banks of the Danube. We walked through the town early one morning and were surprised at the total-- not just the indifference of the population but the expression of hatred towards us and the feeling that this is right. All of these prisoners are dangerous animals and should be exterminated. We walked to the railway station, which was actually quite a distance, especially for people who were hungry and not in good physical condition. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] We were shipped to a relatively small distance along the Danube, to a town called Melk, which is the seat of a beautiful Benedictine monastery that served as a major hospital during the war. The camp that we walked to from the railway station was on the opposite hill of this monastery.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what struck me particularly was that we walked through the-- you would call it kind of a suburban setting of this little town, where little villas lined the street, there was little gardens. And even though this was during the inclement season of the year, winter, one could sense the kind of normalcy of life of these-- in these little houses, fairly small streets of these houses, where you could readily see into them. And this feeling of helplessness again reoccurred, of why us, why are we the ones out here in the street, being herded like cattle, while these other people are living normal life?

speaker: interviewee
text: Normal in comparison with ours, I mean this was towards the end of the war. By that time, anybody in his normal mind should have realized that the war was lost. But Austrians were sympathetic to the Nazi cause and didn't feel anything wrong, seeing us treated in the way that the guards did. That we-- once we were in the camp, we were--

speaker: interviewer
text: What kind of camp was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: This was a work camp. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] Most of the men-- these were almost exclusively men. I don't remember any women. Although some of the history claims that there were some. I don't remember any. Nearby were underground factories for ammunitions that were hulled out in the-- in the mountainside, so that they would be better protected from air raids. And the factories and the-- the caves were all built by the prisoners. And this was very, very strenuous work. And each shift-- they were working in three shifts-- each shift had to work-- walk down to the station, through this cute little street. So these inhabitants saw the prisoners march by three times to work and three times back, literally six times a day. Each shift brought back many dead that died during their shift. They also had to wait at the railway station for quite a while in inclement weather, in both directions. So the death rate was very high. There was a small crematorium also, to deal with the dead. And the very sick were periodically shipped to Mauthausen for extermination. Mauthausen did have a gas chamber. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] The group of boys that was interspersed with the rest of the men came for work there was assigned various tasks. I recall that most of the time, we were sitting in some damp, dark space, peeling rotten potatoes that were mostly blue. Part of the time I was employed as an orderly for a group of French electricians that were responsible for all the lighting, including electrified fences and some other work outside the camp for the Germans. And they treated me very well. And I wish that long after the war I would have been able to connect with any of those survivors. Interestingly, when we were in Mauthausen last year, there was a large group of French people who came. A couple of them were survivors, and a few were wives or children of survivors. And they told us that they came back to Mauthausen every year as the commemoration. And they were in Melk as well. But I was there only for a short time, unable to really make any direct connections. The camp, which was a converted army barrack, and is now serving again for-- for the Austrian Garrison overlooked the Danube-- or-- or sorry, overlooked the valley of the Danube. And by that time, we could see daily air raids by silver, large bombers that would be visible, high in the sky. But even more impressive were the British fighter planes that would suddenly swoop on transports of trains-- the train line was visible from the camp. And they would actually precede the sirens. They would suddenly arrive before the sirens were able to sound. And that was some grief for us to see all the trains that were bringing refugees from-- I mean, inhabitants from Vienna, trying to escape the Russian advance. [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] I had also a job as an orderly, standing next to the main gate. And that was very unpleasant, because I had to watch the shifts going in and out and saw the suffering people, from close proximity.

speaker: interviewer
text: I want to go back just for a moment. You mentioned the glee that you all felt, upon seeing the British fighters and other things happening. What was the response of the Germans while this was happening?

speaker: interviewee
text: I don't know, no idea. I mean, we were not in contact with the Austrian population. And the German guards, I would say that they were beastly till the very end and never changed their attitude towards the prisoners.

speaker: interviewer
text: Thanks.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were in-- in Melk about two months. And probably because of the advancing front from the east, we were then again shipped westward, towards Mauthausen. And that was our second arrival in Mauthausen. This time it was getting extremely crowded. And after a few nights in the main camp, we were pushed to a hastily erected tent camp, which consisted of several almost circus tents, large tents, that were on a slope during the rainy season. And the constant rains were just flooding the place. There was no dry place to be. There was not enough space to be even under the tent, under the protection of the tent. And it was extremely crowded. And there were no blankets and no food. There were occurrences preceding our-- our stay, just a few days where it's claimed that the Germans, not the Allies, dropped a bomb on the camp and that people were actually eating human flesh, out of desperation.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you-- do you specifically remember this?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, this-- this happened a few days before we arrived there.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: We do-- I do recall that one of the air raids, which we watched or saw on the horizon, an American Flying Fortress was shot down. And we could see parachutes floating down and armored vehicles going down into the woods. And we were later told that the fliers were shot, that the Germans killed them. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewee
text: Again, this seemed like a very long time to be in these conditions. And several of the original boys were still hanging together, always trying to form a group, reform groups, so that we would feel a little bit protected. Because by now, the major nationality in this particular camp were Hungarian Jews that were coming. Some of them fresh from their homes in Hungary, because the Germans left the Hungarians alone for a long time. And only towards the end of the war, many of them were sent to concentration camps. There were exceptions. There were actually a large contingent of Hungarian transports that were sent to the gas chambers in the mid-1944, as well.

speaker: interviewer
text: You mentioned that with the arrival of the Hungarian Jewish prisoners, your group tried to stay together that much more.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because nationalities tended to protect each other against the other and gain advantages, whether it was blankets or a dry spot in the tent. There was-- unfortunately, in these extreme conditions, human beings don't always behave like gentlemen. And just like a mother tries to protect her own child [BACKROUND NOISE] before giving shelter to a foreigner, these groups acted similarly and in a way helped orphans, if I can call myself that, or those unprotected children, to sort of form almost a gang that if somebody could steal half a loaf of bread or a slice of bread or a pair of gloves, they would bring it to the group. And this way, there was also a feeling that you were part of something. I think the loneliness and uniqueness of the situation is much more scary than if you know that somebody else is with you. It's like if suddenly the electricity is turned off, you always look on the street. And if I see that whole street is dark, I feel much better than if it's just my house.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were now back in Mauthausen.

speaker: interviewee
text: We were now back in Mauthausen, on a-- a wet, slippery slope in tents. One day, we were suddenly ordered to keep moving, down the hill, to a opening in the [BACKROUND NOISE] [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] barbed wire fence and counted in batches of, say, 100 and guards assigned to us. And we were marched out of Mauthausen. By this time, our guards were mostly older men that were just recruited, for lack of able-bodied soldiers that were all at the front or in other jobs. And we were sent on a march, not knowing where we are going. We went again through the town. By that time, the town looked a little grimmer and less prosperous and was full of military and other vehicles. Streets were clogged with refugees. And these proud Austrians no longer looked quite so proud as they did on our trip a few months earlier. We crossed the Danube on the-- I think the same railway bridge-- because I don't recall that there was any other bridge standing at that time-- and moved away from the Danube on the other bank. And this was our second and fortunately last death march that lasted three days and two nights. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] We were mixed in to-- with the Austrian Jews. There were again guards that were ordered to shoot those who couldn't keep up with the group. And it was a fairly disorderly kind of procession that went through the countryside and through little villages and little towns. The first day was extremely hot, and we were extremely thirsty. And there were instances where we tried-- the prisoners tried to get water at farmhouses. And the farmers would chase them away. There were field kitchens. And in the evening, we did get some soup and some bread. We got--

speaker: interviewer
text: I'm sorry. did you say field kitchens?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, they were on-- on wheels. They were military kitchens that preceded us, that cooked some sort of soup. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] I recall the second night, we all stayed in a-- outside, in a meadow, in a field. And the last day and a half, the weather changed, and it was just pouring incessantly. And we were completely drenched and apathetic and really felt helpless and at the end of our strengths and lacked the will to-- to continue. But something [BACKROUND NOISE] kept us going, in the hope that we will indeed arrive in a dry place and perhaps be able to lie down and get something to eat. [PAUSES FOR 8 SECONDS] We [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS] did not know exactly where we were going. And the third day, we arrived in a forest, [PAUSES FOR 5 SECONDS] which we were told is the end of our destination. And there were a few [BACKROUND NOISE] roughly hewn kind of barracks that were deep in the little forest. Without much light penetrating there, it was extremely damp. And the outside of the barracks were huge puddles.

speaker: interviewer
text: So there was again no dry space. There just wasn't enough room in those barracks for all of us. And people could not lie down.

speaker: interviewee
text: And it was difficult even to sit down for everybody. And there were fights between nationalities, for every inch of space. The people who were close to death were literally pushed out into the puddles outside and died there. [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] Nobody-- there was no organization. Nobody distributed food or water. And the desperation of finally arriving at the place, which we hoped would better our condition, turning out to be so desolate. And that's what I meant, that conditions were gradually worse than-- Auschwitz was worse than Terezin. Mauthausen was worse than Auschwitz. This was really the worst, because there wasn't anything and no hope. And there were rumors that the war is over, that Hitler died. We are now at the end of April, 1945. Indeed, most of Europe by that time has been liberated. And the Germans were still holding out in actually a very small area of Austrian Germany. We were, in fact, liberated only a-- a day or so before the armistice was declared, before the capitulation was signed, which we of course didn't know then. There were-- there was a night when rumors came that the Germans have left, that we are free. And in the morning, when we ventured out, this was not true. But finally, the rumor proved to be substantiated. The Germans have left.

speaker: interviewer
text: How did you know that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Prior to that, there were rumors that there was some Red Cross parcels to be distributed instead of food. And there were terrible fights. And one of the-- one of our group was able to get a parcel so that we had at least a little snack each. We divided it. At one-- another time, they distributed bread, and one of the boys was able to get a loaf of bread for all of us. We shared it. But [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] there were no sanitary facilities. And the sun never reached the place, so it was very depressing. And we were all so-- many of us were now really sick. And had the war not ended, [INAUDIBLE] most of us would not have lived another week.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yourself too? Is that true for you?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Again, I spoke very often about my mother. And the fact that she would not be able to live if I-- she came home and I did not. So that gave me some courage to endure.

speaker: interviewer
text: You said that one day you had heard these rumors that the Germans were no longer there. And they kept on proving to be false rumors. And then one day, sure enough, they were no longer there. What actually happened?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we were sort of isolated in the woods, it was impossible to have an overview of what's happening. And we were really too weak, to just run around freely. And so one of us would sometimes go out and listen to what was being said or reconnoiter or go to what was once the kitchen, to see what was happening. And this is where the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Were there guards there, thought, at that time, German guards?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, yes, oh yes. We were being guarded by German guards, surrounded by a fence. When we-- when we were told-- again, I didn't see it by my own-- own eyes-- that the guards have left, it was towards evening, and some prisoners would stream out of the camp. But we were too weak and too tired. And we decided to stay one more night and only go out of the camp the following day. And that was, to my recollection, May 5.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what happened to the German guards that were guarding you?

speaker: interviewee
text: They left. And what we have learned later is that they had orders to lock us all up in barracks and set fire to the barracks. That was the order from the Gestapo, from the higher up. But the commander apparently was negotiating with the American troops that were closing in and exchanged his so-called freedom for not setting fire to the-- to the barracks.

speaker: interviewer
text: So they put up a white flag, piled into some trucks, and drove who knows where. And so all of a sudden, there was just the--

speaker: interviewee
text: Just the prisoners were left.

speaker: interviewer
text: Just [? left. ?]

speaker: interviewer
text: And again, there was the contrast between many of the Hungarian Jews who haven't been two or three years in concentration camps, so they were in much better physical condition, and even some of them still had some food that they brought with you, not much. Also had reasonable clothes that they were wearing. So there was a-- a distinction between what the endurance that was left in human beings that played a role in survival.

speaker: interviewer
text: And did the Hungarian Jews and the other Jews still separate themselves?

speaker: interviewee
text: I think we were one of the small exceptions. Most of them were Hungarians. And these were both men and women. [BACKROUND NOISE]

speaker: interviewer
text: And how many at this point now of your original group are there?

speaker: interviewee
text: About 15.

speaker: interviewer
text: You tell your story well.
